question: 1. Which of the following characterizes telogen hair?

choices.A: ~60% of hairs in this phase at any time

explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Duration of phase is ~ 3 months. Explanation: Duration of phase is ~ 3 months. Telogen
phase is a “resting” phase lasting approximately 3 months. Approximately 15% of hair is in
this phase at any one time.
Incorrect Answers:
A. ~85% of hairs in this phase at any time is seen in anagen hair.
B. Hair Growth Phase is during the anagen phase.
D. Inner Root Sheath is lost during the catagen phase.
E. Bulb regression occurs during the catagen phase.

question: 2. Patient with wooly hair, PPK, and right-sided cardiomyopathy has what protein defect?
explanation:
Correct answer: (A) Plakoglobin.
Explanation: Naxos disease is an AR disorder due to mutations in plakoglobin resulting in
diffuse palmoplantar keratoderma with wooly hair and right-sided cardiomyopathy.
Incorrect Answers:
B. Desmogleins are desmosomal glycoproteins that are targeted by autoantibodies in
pemphigus. Anti-desmoglein 1 antibodies are implicated in pemphigus foliaceous, while
anti-desmoglein 3 antibodies are implicated in pemphigus vulgaris.
C. Plectin mutations are implicated in EBS with muscular dystrophy.
D. Periplakin (190kDa) is one of several antigenic targets in paraneoplastic pemphigus.
Others include desmoplakin (250 kDa), BPAg1 (230kDa), envoplakin (210 kDa), and plectin
(500 kDa).
E. Envoplakin, like periplakin, is an antigenic target in paraneoplastic pemphigus.
Reference:
Dermatology

question: 3. These nail changes and skin findings can be seen in what condition?
explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Keratosis follicularis.
Explanation: Darier’s disease (keratosis follicularis) presents with alternating longitudinal
red and white streaks and distal fissuring (V-shaped nicks).
Incorrect Answers:
A. Psoriasis more typically presents with irregular nail pitting, onycholysis, and salmon
patches or ‘oil spots’.
B. Tinea presents with nail thickening, yellowish discoloration, subungual debris, and
increased nail fragility.
D. Transient acantholytic dyskeratosis (Grover’s disease) is a distractor. This condition
presents with intensely pruritic papules on the chest, abdomen, and back. No characteristic
nail changes are association with this disorder.
E. Pachyonychia congenita presents with nail thickening and increased transverse curvature
along with palmoplantar keratoderma.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012Image:

question: 4. The patient below presented to you with an enlarging plaque with hair loss, follicular drop-out at the vertex, and perifollicular erythema. Which of the following statements is FALSE?

choices.B: Associated with follicular spines and scarring
choices.C: Lichenoid interface dermatitis centered along the follicular infundibulum
choices.D: May be treated with antimalarials, intralesional or topical steroids

explanation:
Lichen planopilaris (LPP) is an inflammatory cicatricial alopecia that predominantly affects
females.
Incorrect Answers:
B. LPP classically presents with perifollicular erythema, follicular hyperkeratosis (spines),
and scarring.
C. Histologically, LPP is associated with a lichenoid interface dermatitis with a
lymphohistiocytic infiltrate centered along the follicular infundibulum (vs lupus where the
lymphohistiocytic infiltrate is centered along the isthmus).
D. While LPP has a tendency to the resistant to treatment, variable responses have been
reported with plaquenil and corticosteroids (topical, intralesional, and oral).
Reference:
Lichen planopilaris.

question: 5. All of the following hair shaft abnormalities are associated with increased fragility, except:

choices.E: All of the above are associated with increased hair fragility

correct_text: All of the above are associated with increased hair fragility
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
All of the above are associated with increased hair fragility.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Trichorrhexis invaginata (‘bamboo hair’, or ‘ball and socket hair’) is classically seen in
Netherton syndrome, and is associated with increased fragility.
B. Pili torti (twisted hairs) is classically associated with Menkes’ Kinky Hair syndrome, and is
associated with increased hair fragility.
C. Trichorrhexis nodosa (‘broomstick’ hairs) may be seen in arginosuccinic aciduria,
citrullinemia, and Menkes’ Kinky Hair syndrome. This abnormality is associated with
increased hair fragility.
D. Monilethrix (‘beaded’ hair) presents with short brittle hair due to increased hair fragility.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012
http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23461057

question: 6. Which of the following nail associations is incorrect?

choices.D: red and white longitudinal streaks - Darier’s

explanation:
Correct answer: (B)
Ventral pterygium - lichen planus.
Explanation: Lichen planus typically presents with dorsal pterygium. Ventral pterygium is
associated with scleroderma. Ventral pterygium is characterized by the adhesion of the
distal nail plate to the hyponychium.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Lichen planus presents with trachyonychia (roughness due to excessive longitudinal
ridging). Severe cases may presents with sandpapered-appearing nails that can affect all
finger- and toenails.
C. Nail pitting can be seen in psoriasis, alopecia area, and lichen planus. Pits are caused by
abnormal keratinization of the proximal nail matrix that produce parakeratotic clusters along
the dorsal nail plate.
D. Darier’s classically presents with v-nicking along the distal nail plate and alternating red
and white longitudinal streaks.
E. Spoon nails, or koilonychia, is associated with iron deficiency. Spoon nails are thinned,
concave nails due to upward eversion of the lateral edges of the nail.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 7. Which of the following is least likely to cause blue-grey discoloration of the nails?
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
Amiodarone
Explanation: Amiodarone can produce blue-grey skin pigmentation in sun-exposed areas
(face and hands). Bluish discoloration of the nails is not an expected side effect.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Blue or azure lunula is frequently caused by zidovudine in patients with HIV.
B. Wilson’s disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) may present with blue lunulae.
C. Argyria presents with blue to bluish grey skin changes which can affect skin and mucosal
areas.
D. Minocycline is associated with blue-grey discoloration of the nail plate.

question: 8. Which of the following hair shaft abnormality associations is incorrect?
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
All of the statements are correct.
Correct Answer Explanations:
A. Pili torti (twisted hairs) is seen in Menkes’ Kinky Hair syndrome, an X-linked recessive
disorder of copper metabolism due to ATP7A mutations. Severe psychomotor retardation,
growth failure, seizures, and other neurologic abnormalities are also seen in this condition.
B. Trichorrhexis invaginata (‘bamboo hair’) is commonly seen in association with
Netherton’s syndrome.
C. Trichothiodystrophy is an AR disorder characterized by photosensitivity, ichthyosis,
brittle hair, intellectual impairment, decreased fertility, and short stature (PIBIDS). Under
polarizing microscopy, the hair shows alternating bright and dark bands that give a tiger-tail
appearance.
D. Pili triangulati et canaliculi is seen in Uncombable Hair syndrome. Microscopically, the
hair shafts have a triangular cross-sectional appearance with a longitudinal groove.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 9. All of the following may cause photo-onycholysis except:
explanation:
Correct answer: (B)
Amiodarone
Explanation: Amiodarone is associated with blue-grey discoloration of the skin in a photo
distributed pattern. It is not associated with photo-onycholysis.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Tetracyclines are most commonly implicated in drug-induced photo-onycholysis.
C. Photo-onycholysis is a characteristic side-effect of photodynamic therapy.
D. Photo-onycholysis is a possible side-effect of fluoroquinolones.
E. Psoralens are associated with photo-onycholysis. Photo-onycholysis is a characteristic
side-effect of tetracyclines, taxanes, psoralens, and photodynamic therapy.
Reference:
Baran R, Juhlin L. Photoonycholysis. Photodermatology, Photoimmunology & Photomedicine. Vol 33 (6
Issues in 2017).

question: 10. Which describes shedding of the nail plate at the proximal nail fold?
explanation:
Correct answer: (B)
Onychomadesis.
Explanation: Onychomadesis is characterized by detachment of the nail plate from the
proximal nail fold due to nail matrix trauma.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Onycholysis occurs when the nail plate is detached from the nail bed. This produces
whitish discoloration due to air present in the subungual space.
C. Onychoschizia or nail splitting or brittleness are commonly seen, and typically due to
dehydration of the nail plate from environmental factors like frequent handwashing.
D. Onychorrhexis is characterized by longitudinal ridging and fissuring of the nail plate.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 11. A patient presents with increased transverse curvature of multiple fingerand toenails where the lateral edges approach one another. This would be seen in which of the following?
explanation:
Correct answer: (A)
Pachyonychia congenita.
Explanation: Transverse curvature of the nails describes a pincer nail deformity, which is
seen in Pachyonychia Congenita. Severe nail bed hyperkeratosis underlies nail thickening
and increased transverse curvature seen in this condition.
Incorrect Answers:
B. Dyskeratosis congenita presents with longitudinal ridging and splitting, followed by
pterygium formation.
C. Tinea is a distractor, and typically presents with nail thickening, yellowish discoloration,
and increased fragility.
D. Psoriasis presents with nail pitting, ridging, and ‘oil spots’ (translucent yellowish-red
discoloration underneath the nail bed).
E. Patients can have different degrees of nail displasia in Nail-Patella Syndrome, but
triangular lunulae are pathognomonic.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 12. Which of the following is false regarding duration of hair cycle phases?

choices.C: Terminal hairs on the legs have a longer anagen cycle duration than terminal hairs on the arms
choices.E: Plucking hairs is not associated with premature induction of anagen phase

correct_text: Plucking hairs is not associated with premature induction of anagen phase
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
Plucking hairs is not associated with premature induction of anagen phase.
Explanation: Plucking hairs (depilation) is highly associated with premature anagen
induction.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Anagen (growth) phase is the longest phase and typically lasts 2-6 years.
B. Catagen (involution) phase is the shortest phase and typically lasts 2-3 weeks.
C. Terminal hairs of the legs have an anagen duration of 19-26 weeks, as opposed to 6-12
weeks associated with terminal hairs on the arms.
D. Estrogens prolong anagen phase, while corticosteroids retard onset of anagen phase.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 13. Which of the following is false?

choices.A: Dark hair has a predominance of eumelanin
choices.B: Red hair has a predominance of pheomelanin
choices.C: Hair fiber strength is largely due to disulfide bonding

explanation:
Correct Answer:
D. Estrogens increase hair growth rate.
Estrogens increase hair growth rate.
Explanation: Estrogens slow rate of hair growth but increase during the anagen phase,
therefore choice (D) is the false statement.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Darkly pigmented hair has a predominance of eumelanin (which imparts a brown to black
hue).
B. Pheomelanin imparts a pink to red hue, and is therefore the predominant melanin type
associated with red hair.
C. Strong disulfide bonds are responsible for hair fiber strength.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 14. Miniaturization of hair follicles is histologically most characteristic for which type of alopecia?
explanation:
Correct answer: (B)
Androgenetic alopecia.
Explanation: Androgenetic alopecia (AGA, or male and female pattern hair loss) has a
normal total number of follicles and no significant inflammation. Increased number of
miniaturized hairs is characteristic.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Alopecia areata is histologically characterized by a peribulbar lymphohistiocytic infiltrate.
Miniaturized hairs can be found in longstanding disease, however peribulbar inflammation is
more characteristic for AA.
C. Lichen planopilaris does not demonstrate miniaturization of hair follicles, but rather
vacuolar alteration at the basement membrane and a lymphohistiocytic infiltrate centered
along the follicular infundibulum.
D. Trichotillomania is associated with normal hair follicle size, increased catagen hairs, and
trichomalacia with pigment casts.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 15. Which of the following is FALSE regarding the condition below? Note" Trichoscopic findings are significant for reductions in hair density, hair diameter, empty follicles, and vellus hairs with loss of follicular openings.

choices.A: Occurs with higher frequency in African-Americans
choices.B: Associated with a history of tight braids, curlers, chemical relaxer use

explanation:
Correct Answer:
C. Is not associated with scarring.
Is not associated with scarring.
Chronic end-stage traction alopecia is associated with fibrosis.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Traction alopecia typically presents in African-American women.
B. Hair trauma, including a history of tight braids, curlers, or chemical relaxation is
associated with the disorder.
D. A & B are both true statements.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012
Image: By self (Own work) [GFDL (http://www.gnu.org/copyleft/fdl.html) or CC-BY-SA-3.0
(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)], via Wikimedia Commons

question: 16. Which of the following hair disorder associations is incorrect?

choices.E: None. All of the above are correct

explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Pili torti : plakoglobin.
Explanation: Pili torti can be associated with Menkes disease and Bjornstad syndrome,
neither of which are associated with plakoglobin mutations. Mutations in plakoglobin are
associated with woolly hair, as seen in Naxos disease.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Monilethrix is associated with KRT81 and KRT86 mutations (previously referred to as
hHb1 and hHb6).
B. Trichorrhexis invaginata is characteristically seen in Netherton syndrome, which is due to
SPINK5 mutation.
D. Woolly hair is seen in Carvajal syndrome (desmoplakin mutations), as well as Naxos
disease (plakoglobin mutations).
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 17. Hypertrichosis is a feature of which congenital syndromes?
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
All of the statements are true.
A. Cornelia de Lange presents with hypertrichosis on the face, forehead, shoulders and
back. Clinically, patients may present with synophrys and a low anterior hairline.
B. Rubinstein-Taybi presents with hypertrichosis on the lateral face, shoulders and back.
C. Variegate Porphyria presents with hypertrichosis along the lateral face.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 18. Longitudinal melanonychia is associated with which of the following?
explanation:
Correct answer: (E)
All of the statements are true.
B. Drugs are a common cause for longitudinal melanonychia, including AZT (zidovudine),
doxorubicin, 5-FU, and psoralens.
C. Addison’s disease may present with longitudinal melanonychia due to melanocyte
activation.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 19. Which of the following nail findings would you expect for the condition below?
explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Trachyonychia.
Explanation: Longitudinal ridging leading to nail roughness (or trachyonychia) is associated
with lichen planus. Involvement of all nails (twenty nail dystrophy) is a presentation of lichen
planus in children. Advanced nail disease in lichen planus may result in dorsal pterygium.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Irregular nail pitting is commonly associated with psoraisis. Nail pits are due to abnormal
keritinzation of the nail matrix
B. Geometric nail pitting is commonly associated with alopecia areata.
D. Longitudinal red and white streaks are associated with Darier’s disease, not lichen
planus.
E. Pincer nails (increased transverse curvature) are associated with Pachyonychia
Congenita.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 20. All of the following are characteristic of telogen effluvium, except?

choices.B: May be precipitated by infections, stress, hypothyroidism, anticonvulsants and antihypertensive medications
choices.C: Hair loss usually begins immediately after a precipitating event
choices.D: Eventual spontaneous hair regrowth is expected
choices.E: None. All of the above are true

correct_text: Hair loss usually begins immediately after a precipitating event
explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Hair loss usually begins immediately after a precipitating event.
Explanation:
Hair loss usually begins 3 months after the identified precipitating factor (if one exists).
Incorrect Answers:
A. Telogen effluvium is associated with increased telogen counts > 20% (>15% is
suggestive).
B. Telogen effluvium may be physiologic (postpartum), or due to infections, stress, an
endocrinopathy (thyroid or parathyroid disease), medications (B-blockers, retinoids,
antithyroid medications, anticonvulsants), or crash dieting.
D. Eventual spontaneous regrowth is the expected outcome, but may take months to years.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 21. Which of the following is true regarding this condition?

choices.A: May be associated with autoimmune thyroid disease
choices.B: May be treated with intralesional kenalog injections or topical steroids
choices.C: Behavior modification is the mainstay of therapy

correct_text: Behavior modification is the mainstay of therapy
explanation:
Correct answer: (C)
Behavior modification is the mainstay of therapy.
Explanation: Trichotillomania is difficult to treat and may include behavior modification,
psychotherapy, and antidepressant medications like SSRIs.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Trichotillomania characteristically presents as a discrete patch of alopecia with varying
lengths of hair. Alopecia area (AA) is included in the differential of discrete non-scarring hair
loss, however AA would not present with hairs of varying length. AA, not trichotillomania, is
associated with autoimmune disease.
B. Corticosteroids are not recommended treatments for trichotillomania.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 22. A 45 year-old-male patient presents with the findings shown below. He noted some mild hair loss in these areas a month ago, and over the following week, all of the hair there fell out. He denies any trauma to the sites and before the hair loss, had no symptoms. During the hair shedding, he did have some mild itching. On exam, the underlying and adjacent skin exhibits no abnormalities, aside from the hair loss. Which of the following statements is correct?

choices.A: ANA, thyroid function, and serum B12 should always be checked.
choices.B: Melanin pigment casts are pathognomonic for this disorder.
choices.C: Psychiatric evaluation should be considered in refractory cases.

explanation:
ANSWER: D.
Alopecia areata (pictured) is a non-scarring condition that presents with discrete circular
patches of hair loss. While the disease course is variable, spontaneous recovery is possible.
The condition most often occurs on the scalp, though it may occur on any hair-bearing
area.
Incorrect Answers:
A. While alopecia areata is associated with other autoimmune disorders like autoimmune
thyroid disease (Hashimoto’s), vitiligo, and inflammatory bowel disease, the extent of the
autoimmune workup should be dictated by the patient’s overall symptoms and relevant
family history.
B. Melanin pigment casts are found in both trichotillomania and alopecia areata.
C. Trichotillomania may mimic alopecia areata since both processes can produce discrete
patches of alopecia. However, trichotillomania creates unusual broken hair patterns of
varying lengths compared with the smooth hair loss of alopecia areata. Patients with
trichotillomania, not alopecia areata, should be referred to psychiatry for evaluation in
refractory cases.
Vital concept:
Alopecia areata produces non-scarring areas of hair loss. It is believed to have an
autoimmune cause.
Reference:
1. Bolognia JL, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set: Expert Consult Premium Edition Enhanced Online Features and Print, 3rd ed. Saunders 2012.
2. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/alopecia-areata-clinical-manifestations-and-diagnosis

question: 23. On examination of a child with sensorineural deafness, you notice hair that appears twisted on itself, which you determine is consistent with pili torti. The genetic disorder that this patient most likely has is characterized by which of the following:

choices.C: a mutation in the ATP7A gene
choices.D: a mutation in a copper transporting ATPase

explanation:
Correct answer: (A) Autosomal recessive inheritance.
This patient has Bjornstad syndrome, a defect in the gene BSC1L, a component of the inner
mitochondrial membrane, which phenotypically is characterized by sensorineural deafness
and pili torti.
Incorrect Answers:
B-E. All describe Menkes' Kinky Hair syndrome.

question: 24. Which of the following is incorrectly paired?

choices.A: Pili trianguli et canaliculi: Uncombable hair syndrome
choices.D: Wooly hair: Menkes Kinky Hair syndrome

correct_text: Wooly hair: Menkes Kinky Hair syndrome
explanation:
Correct answer: (D) Wooly hair: Menkes Kinky Hair syndrome.
Wooly hair can occur as a feature of Naxos syndrome, Carvajal syndrome, as a wooly hair
nevus, and can be inherited in an autosomal recessive form with a mutation in the P2RY5
gene. It is not a characteristic feature of Menkes Kinky Hair syndrome, whose characteristic
hair abnormalities include pili torti, monilethrix and trichorrhexis nodosa.
Incorrect Answers:
A, B, C & E. All are correctly paired.

question: 25. The insect below was most likely recovered from which of the following locations?
explanation:
Correct answer: (A) Scalp.
Head lice, or pediculosis capitis, demonstrated in the photo above, are obligate human
parasites that feed on host blood. The lice themselves are limited to the scalp, behind the
ears and the lower portions of the neck.
Incorrect Answers:
B-E. Lice prefer the warm environment of the scalp as well as the proximity to a rich
vascular supply for them to feed. They also require the longer and straighter hair shafts
found on the scalp that allow them to lay and attach their eggs.
Reference:
Infestations. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012

question: 26. All of the statements regarding the illustration below are true EXCEPT?

choices.A: Eggs are laid close to the scalp
choices.B: Eggs easily slide off the hair shaft
choices.D: When full grown these insects can survive for 36 hours away from their host

correct_text: Eggs easily slide off the hair shaft
explanation:
Correct answer: (B) Eggs easily slide off the hair shaft.
Lice, pediculosis capitis, lay their eggs approximately 1 cm away from the scalp.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Lice secrete a proteinaceous material to firmly adhere the eggs to the hair shaft. These
lesions are not mobile and do not easily slide on the hair shaft. This should not be confused
with the superficial fungal infection causing white piedra, in which similar appearing lesions
attached to the hair shaft are mobile. Dried hair products, or hair casts are also mobile and
should not be confused for eggs. The remaining choices are true.
C. Infestation can be asymptomatic and the absence of pruritus should not rule out the
diagnosis. Many “carriers” do not present with any symptoms.
D. Full-grown lice can spend up to 36 hours away from their human host without a blood
meal.
E. Transmission occurs in one of two ways, direct head-to-head contact and more
commonly through fomites. These tend to be brushes, combs, headgear or hats, but can
also occur through bedding and blow dryers.
Reference:
Infestations. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012

question: 27. A concerned parent brings his two daughters to your clinic. His wife and daughters have had an itchy scalp rash for the past 3 weeks that has not improved with a topical medication they were prescribed. The parent cannot recall the name of the medicine, but affirms that it was used appropriately as instructed and then repeated a few days later. He is frustrated that the medication did not work and is seeking additional management. Microscopic exam of material removed from the scalp with scotch tape demonstrated the findings below. Which of the following treatments of this infestation has demonstrated no resistance?
explanation:
Correct answer: (E) Ivermectin.
Ivermectin is an orally taken antiparasitic medication that has generated no reports of
resistance anywhere in the world. Studies have demonstrated complete clearance when
400 mcg/kg are given on day 1 and day 8 following presentation in patients who had failed
other topical treatment modalities. Oral Ivermectin is promising as an alternative treatment
to the topical antiparasitics due to the increase in resistance to each of the medications
listed in Choices A-D. Safety data on special populations, children weighing less than 33
pounds, pregnant and breast-feeding women, have not been conducted. These patient
populations should not be administered this medication because of a lack of safety profile.
Reference:
Chosidow O, Giraudeau B, Cottrell, et al. Oral ivermectin versus malathion lotion for difficult-to-treat head
lice. N Engl J Med. 2010;362:896–905.
Infestations. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012

question: 28. Which of the following is the most accurate description of the Exogen phase of the hair cycle?

choices.A: The shedding of the telogen hair from the hair follicle
choices.B: Growth phase of the hair follicle
choices.C: Keratinocyte apoptosis of lower 2/3rds of the hair follicle

correct_text: The shedding of the telogen hair from the hair follicle
explanation:
Correct answer: (A) The shedding of the telogen hair from the hair follicle.
Exogen, a recently adapted hair-cycle term, is defined by two important changes in the hair
cycle. The first is the active process of shedding the club hair from the hair follicle, and the
second represents the initiation of signaling that later leads to the structural changes in the
hair follicle following the active shedding phase.
Incorrect Answers:
B. Defines the Anagen phase of the hair cycle.
C. Defines the Catagen phase of the hair cycle.
D. Defines the Telogen phase of the hair cycle.
E. Defines the Kenogen phase of the hair cycle. This is also an additional phase of the hair
cycle that has been added to the medical literature and describes the period of time in
which the telogen hair follicle is devoid of its club fiber, and has yet to enter the anagen
phase. An increased proportion of hair follicles in androgenetic alopecia are in the Kenogen
phase of the hair cycle.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 29. All of the following statements regarding hair growth during fetal and neonatal development are true EXCEPT:

choices.A: Human hair follicle morphogenesis takes place only once
choices.B: Terminal hairs initially grow in a synchronous and wave-like pattern anteriorly to posteriorly
choices.C: Pigmented lanugo hair is shed in an anterior to posterior wave at 7-8 months of gestation
choices.D: Shorter un-pigmented lanugo hair is shed 3-4 months after birth
choices.E: Pigmented hairs on the scalp are shed in an anterior to posterior wave postnatally

correct_text: Terminal hairs initially grow in a synchronous and wave-like pattern anteriorly to posteriorly
explanation:
Correct answer: (B) Terminal hairs initially grow in a synchronous and wave-like pattern
anteriorly to posteriorly.
Terminal hair growth is asynchronous and occurs in a mosaic pattern. Unlike the growth of
the pigmented and un-pigmented lanugo hair, terminal hair, found mainly on the scalp,
eyebrows, and eyelashes, is not grown in a wave pattern.
Incorrect Answers:
A, C, D & E. These are correct statements. Human hair follicle morphogenesis of vellus,
lanugo and terminal hairs, takes place only once during development. Pigmented lanugo
hair is shed in an anterior to posterior wave in-utero between 7 and 8 months of gestation.
Shorter, unpigmented lanugo hair is shed 3-4 months after birth, while pigmented hairs on
the scalp, are shed in the postnatal period in an anterior to posterior wave similar to
pigmented lanugo hair shedding that takes place during pregnancy.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 30. Epithelial stem cells of hair follicle arise from where?

choices.A: Rapidly proliferating keratinocytes from the hair matrix
choices.B: Lowermost portion of the hair follicle
choices.C: Segment of the outer root sheath located near the insertion of the arrector pili muscle
choices.E: Mesenchymal connective tissue follicular sheath that is attached to the hair follicle basement membrane

correct_text: Segment of the outer root sheath located near the insertion of the arrector pili muscle
explanation:
Correct answer: (C) Segment of the outer root sheath located near the insertion of the
arrector pili muscle.
The hair bulge is a segment of the outer root sheath located at the site of arrector pili
muscle insertion, and is the major source of epithelial stem cells for the hair follicle.
Incorrect Answers:
A. The hair matrix, located near the lower end of the hair follicle, is the site of rapidly
proliferating keratinocytes that eventuate in the production of the hair shaft. Stem cells of
the hair follicle are not located in this region.
B. The hair bulb is the lowermost portion of the hair follicle.
D. The follicular dermal papilla is a tightly packed region of numerous fibroblasts that
communicate with the surrounding connective tissue sheath in order to regulate and induce
hair growth. There is a direct relationship between the volume of the dermal papillae, size of
the hair bulb, and the hair shaft diameter.
E. The connective tissue sheath is tightly attached to the hair follicle basement membrane
and is continuous with the dermal papillae.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 31. Which of the following layers of the hair shaft and hair follicle is CORRECTLY paired with its immediate surrounding layer at the level of the hair bulb?

choices.B: Hair cortex and the Huxley layer of the inner root sheath
choices.C: Huxley layer of the inner root sheath and the cuticle of the hair shaft
choices.D: Henle’s layer of the inner root sheath and the glassy membrane
choices.E: Hair shaft cuticle and inner root sheath cuticle

correct_text: Hair shaft cuticle and inner root sheath cuticle
explanation:
Correct answer: (E) Hair shaft cuticle and inner root sheath cuticle.
The inner root sheath cuticle lies directly external to the hair shaft cuticle. The correct order
in layers starting at the medulla of the hair shaft to the outermost layer of the hair follicle is
as follows: Hair Shaft (medulla → cortex → hair shaft cuticle) → Inner root sheath cuticle →
Huxley’s Layer → Henle’s Layer → Outer root sheath (corresponding to the epidermal
stratum spinosum) → stratum basale or basal layer of the hair follicle → glassy membrane →
and finally the follicular sheath.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 32. At what point during fetal development do the first hair follicles form?
explanation:
Correct answer: (B) 9-10 weeks.
Hair follicles in the fetus develop between weeks 9 and 10 of gestation. At 15-16 weeks hair
shafts begin to occupy the hair follicles (Choice D), and by week 20-22 (Choice E), all scalp
hair is in anagen phase.
Reference:
Eichenfield L, Frieden I, Zaenglein A, Mathes E. Neonatal and Infant Dermatology, 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2015
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 33. A colleague refers a patient to you out of concern for pigmentation underneath several of his fingernails. He presents photos of a 34-year-old male of Hispanic descent with dark bands presenting longitudinally on several of his fingernails. You explain that this is unlikely melanoma. Which of the following nail components contributes to these clinical findings?
explanation:
Correct answer: (B) Nail matrix.
While the pigmented bands are seen in the nail plate, the origin of these findings develops
because of the melanocytes in the nail matrix. Nail matrix melanocytes are found in the
highest density in the distal nail matrix, and in contrast to epidermal melanocytes, can exist
in both basal and suprabasal layers.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Pigmented bands in the nail plate, as demonstrated by the patient in this vignette, are
also called longitudinal melanonychia. This is a normal finding and is commonly found in
patients with darkly pigmented skin.
C. Melanocyte induced pigmentation of the proximal nail fold, also known as the
Hutchinson’s sign, may be a sign of localized melanoma. Pigmentation in this location is
atypical and warrants further investigation.
D. Pigmentation in the hyponychium may result from deposition of melanin in the nail bed
and plate by melanocytes in the nail matrix, but does not originate from this location.
E. Pigment in the nail bed that has no connection to the nail folds or matrix, are not
melanocytic in origin, except in the case of metastatic melanoma.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 34. You are consulted to urgent care for your input on a 12 year-old male patient who presents with exquisite and tender pain originating from the distal part of his middle finger. On examination he presents with significant tenderness, erythema, and some purulent discharge from the lateral nail fold. You presume acute paronychia due to bacteria and recommend a culture with the initiation of empiric antibiotics. Which of the following risk factors is most concerning for bacterial infection spreading to adjacent tissue and bone?

choices.A: Chronic damage and inflammation of the cuticle
choices.B: The moist environment conducive to bacterial and fungal growth
choices.C: The lack of subcutaneous tissue between the nail dermis and bone
choices.D: The high level of vascularization with the distal phalanx

correct_text: The lack of subcutaneous tissue between the nail dermis and bone
explanation:
Correct Answer:
C. The lack of subcutaneous tissue between the nail dermis and bone.
Beneath the nail plate and bed, there is a thin layer of fibrocollagenous dermis separating
the nail apparatus from the distal phalanx. The absence of subcutaneous tissue, including
adipose and adnexal structures, increases the risk of bacterial structure to adjacent bone,
and resulting osteomyelitis.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Chronic damage and inflammation of the cuticle may result in nail fold inflammation and
onychodystrophy secondary to disruptions in the normal maturation of the epithelium,
however, this does not predispose to osteomyelitis.
B. Repeated trauma to the cuticle and submersion/exposure to moist environments
increases the risk for onychomycosis, or fungal nail infection. This typically presents with
mild, chronic pain, following months to years of repeated trauma and submersion, however,
this does not increase the risk for local bacterial spread to bone and ensuing osteomyelitis.
D. Finally, the high level of vascularity within the distal phalanx is not a risk factor
contributing to a local bacterial infection resulting in osteomyelitis.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 35. All of the following statements regarding nail anatomy and growth are true EXCEPT?

choices.A: The proximal nail fold keratinizes with a granular layer
choices.B: The proximal nail fold contains sweat glands
choices.C: The nail matrix keratinizes with a granular layer and gives rise to the nail plate
choices.D: The nail matrix contains melanocytes in the basal and suprabasal layers of its epithelium
choices.E: The nail bed undergoes abrupt keratinization without a granular layer

correct_text: The nail matrix keratinizes with a granular layer and gives rise to the nail plate
explanation:
Correct answer: (C) The nail matrix keratinizes with a granular layer and gives rise to the nail
plate.
The nail matrix does not normally keratinize with a granular layer but does give rise to the
nail plate. Inflammatory conditions such as psoriasis can involve the nail matrix, and
parakeratosis within the nail matrix presents as pitting which is often seen in these patients.
Incorrect Answers:
A, B, D & E. These are all correct.
Reference:
Biology of Hair & Nails. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. ©
Saunders 2012

question: 36. A 54-year-old female presents to your clinic with complaints of scalp pain and itchiness. She is mostly concerned with the localized hair loss illustrated below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Lichen planopilaris.
The areas of the scalp seen in the photo that are devoid of any remnant hair follicles
categorize this condition as a scarring alopecia. A telling feature that will aid the diagnosis is
the perifollicular erythema associated with the scarring alopecia. This clinical finding is
consistent with the diagnosis of lichen planopilaris.
Incorrect Answers:
A and E. Androgenic alopecia and alopecia areata may present with patchy hair loss, but do
not have follicle unit loss and scarring as a feature.
C. Syphilitic alopecia, which presents with a more diffuse and patchy presentation, could be
considered, but rarely leads to the scarring seen in the photograph.
D. While dissecting cellulitis of the scalp is a scarring alopecia, it is characterized by boggy
and indurated plaques of alopecia scattered over the scalp. They may present with some
mild discomfort, but this is often overshadowed by the foul-smelling discharge that can
accompany this condition.
Reference:
Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Alopecias & hair shaft abnormalities. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd
Edition. Saunders. 2012.

question: 37. Which structure of the mature hair follicle is usually located into the subcutis?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Dermal papilla.
All of the structures are located into the dermis, except for the dermal papilla that is usually
located into the subcutis for the mature hair follicle.
Reference:
p. 1076. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 38. Select the phase of the human hair follicle cycle that is the shortest?

choices.E: The phases of the hair follicle cycle are all of the same length.

explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Catagen.
The hair follicle cycle lengths are the following: Anagen – Can last several years (2 to 6
years) Catagen – Typically 2 weeks (2 to 3 weeks) (which is the correct answer) Telogen – 3
months The nanogen term is inexistant in the hair follicle cycle. Therefore, the last answer is
not right, because the phases are all different lengths.
Reference:
p. 1078 to 1082. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012

question: 39. What percentage of the terminal scalp hair is in the anagen phase?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
E. 85%.
The hair cycle distribution for terminal scalp hair: Anagen: 85–90% (correct answer is
included in this interval) Telogen: 10–15% Catagen: <1%
Reference:
p. 1082. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 40. Name the structures of the layers of the hair follicle starting from the most inner component:

choices.A: Cortex, medulla, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Henle's layer, Huxley's layer, companion layer, outer root sheath
choices.B: Medulla, cortex, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Huxley’s layer, Henle’s layer, companion layer, outer root sheath
choices.C: Medulla, cortex, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Henle’s layer, Huxley’s layer, companion layer, outer root sheath
choices.D: Cortex, medulla, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Huxley’s layer, Henle’s layer, companion layer, outer root sheath
choices.E: Medulla, cortex, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Huxley’s layer, Henle’s layer, outer root sheath, companion layer

correct_text: Medulla, cortex, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Huxley’s layer, Henle’s layer, companion layer, outer root sheath
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Medulla, cortex, hair cuticle, inner root sheath cuticle, Huxley’s layer, Henle’s layer,
companion layer, outer root sheath.
The correct order of the layers, starting from the most inner component is:
- Medulla, cortex
- Hair cuticule
- Inner root sheath cuticle
- Huxley’s layer
- Henle’s layer
- Companion layer
- Outer root sheath
Reference:
p. 1084. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 41. Select the primary cicatricial alopecia that is classified as being a neutrophilic type:
explanation:
Correct Answer:
D. Dissecting cellulitis.
Dissecting cellulitis, as well as folliculitis decalvans (not listed in the choices) are classified
as a neutrophilic primary cicatricial alopecia. All other choices are classified as lymphocytic
primary cicatricial alopecias.
Reference:
p. 1105. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 42. Which option best represents the characteristic histopathological findings in acute and subacute alopecia areata?

choices.A: Miniaturization of hair follicles without inflammation
choices.B: Vacuolar interface alteration of the follicular epithelium
choices.C: Miniaturization of hair follicles with a peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate
choices.D: Focal hypergranulosis at the acrotrichium with a dense perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate. Presence of Max-Joseph spaces between the hair follicles and the perifollicular sheath

correct_text: Miniaturization of hair follicles with a peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate
explanation:
Correct Answer:
C. Miniaturization of hair follicles with peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate.
Alopecia areata is histopathologically characterized by the miniaturization of hair follicles
with a peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate.
In chronic alopecia areata there is a decrease in peribulbar inflammation and an increase in
vellus hairs. At its most developed, there may be little or no peribulbar inflammation
Incorrect Answers:
A. The miniaturization of hair follicles without inflammation characterizes androgenetic
alopecia, as well as an increased number of vellous hair.
B. Vacuolar interface alteration of the follicular epithelium is associated with discoid lupus.
D. Focal hypergranulosis at the acrotrichium with a dense perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate
and presence of Max-Joseph spaces between the hair follicles and the perifollicular sheath
evokes the diagnosis of lichen planopilaris.
E. Alopecia areata is not associated with a increased number of vellous hairs.
Vital Concept:
|Alopecia areata is a common autoimmune disorder that often results in unpredictable hair
loss. Characterized histopathologically, alopecia areata is notable for miniaturization of hair
follicles with a peribulbar lymphocytic infiltrate
Reference:
Bolognia, J. L., Jorizzo, J. L., & Schaffer, J. V. (2012). Dermatology (3rd ed.). Saunders.

question: 43. Identify the disorder associated with a mutation in Keratin 81 (KRT81) in the following choices:
explanation:
Correct Answer:
A. Monilethrix.
Monilethrix can be caused by mutations in the following: Keratin 81, keratin 83, keratin 86.
In these 3 cases, the disease will be transmitted in an autosomal dominant fashion. If the
mutation concerns desmoglein 4, the disease will be transmitted in an autosomal recessive
fashion. A mutation in connexin 26 can cause keratitis, ichthyosis and deafness (KID
syndrome). Menkes disease is related to a mutation in the Cu2+-transporting P-type
ATPase 7A. Björnstad syndrome is caused by a mutation involving BCS1-like (mitochondrial
membrane protein). Netherton syndrome is associated with a mutation in Serine protease
inhibitor LEKTI encoded by the SPINK5 gene.
Reference:
p. 1104. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 44. Which of the following conditions is best characterized by a slowly progressive, symmetrical and cicatricial alopecia centered on the crown or vertex which is most often found in black women of African descent? Hair care practices, such as the use of the hot comb, relaxers, tight extensions and weaves, have been implicated for decades, but studies have not shown a consistent link. Other proposed causative factors include fungal infections, bacterial infections, autoimmune disease, and genetics.
explanation:
Correct Answer:
A. Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia (CCCA).
All the listed conditions are cicatricial forms of alopecia. Central centrifugal cicatricial
alopecia is characterized by a slowly progressive, symmetrical and cicatricial alopecia
centered on the crown or vertex which is most often found in black women of African
descent.
Hair care practices, such as the use of the hot comb, relaxers, tight extensions and weaves,
have been implicated for decades, but studies have not shown a consistent link. Other
proposed causative factors include fungal infections, bacterial infections, autoimmune
disease, and genetics.
Incorrect Answers:
B. Lichen planopilaris is an inflammatory, cicatricial alopecia with various patterns of hair
loss. It is the most common cause of Brocq’s alopecia (or end-stage cicatricial alopecia).
One form is called frontal fibrosing alopecia and is a distinctive clinical subset. It can be
very resistant to treatment.
C. Discoid lupus is not linked to the use of caustic hair products, nor are they linked to
dissecting cellulitis of the scalp.
D. Acne keloidalis is most common in black men, but can occur in women and Caucasians. It
is a primary form of cicatricial alopecia and often occurs in conjunction with central
centrifugal cicatricial alopecia.
E. Dissecting cellulitis, also known as perifolliculitis capitis abscedens et suffodiens or
Hoffman disease, is an inflammatory disease of the skin. It manifests as perifollicular
pustules, keloids, nodules, abscesses, and sinuses that evolve into scarring alopecia.
Reference:
p. 1105 to 1107. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012
Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia: challenges and solutions.

question: 45. The follicle occlusion tetrad is composed of which four (4) conditions?

choices.A: Lichen planopilaris, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and pilonidal sinus.
choices.B: Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and vellous hair cysts.
choices.C: Acne keloidalis, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and pilonidal sinus.
choices.D: Acne keloidalis, pilar cysts, steatocystomas and Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp.
choices.E: Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and pilonidal sinus.

correct_text: Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and pilonidal sinus.
explanation:
Correct Answer:
E. Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp, acne conglobata, hidradenitis suppurativa and
pilonidal sinus.
The follicle occlusion tetrad is composed of the following: - Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp
- Acne conglobata - Hidradenitis suppurativa - Pilonidal sinus
Reference:
p. 1108. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders 2012

question: 46. In Naxos disease (diffuse non-epidermolytic PPK; due to plakoglobin mutations) and Carvajal syndrome (striate epidermolytic PPK; due to desmoplakin mutations), what hair finding is classically found?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Woolly hair.
Naxos disease (diffuse non-epidermolytic PPK; due to plakoglobin mutations) and Carvajal
syndrome (striate epidermolytic PPK; due to desmoplakin mutations) both have woolly hair
as a characteristic hair finding.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Spunglass hair is also known as pili trianguli et canaliculi and is due to the abnormal
keratinization of the internal root sheath. It causes uncombable hair.
C. Pili multigemini is associated with multiple hair arising from the same papilla but is not
associated with syndromes.
D. Trichorrhexis invaginata is associated with Netherton syndrome.
E. Pili torti is a feature of Björnstad syndrome.
Reference:
p. 1111 and 1112. Bolognia J, Jorizzo JL, Schaffer JV. Dermatology: 2-Volume Set 3rd Edition. © Saunders
2012

question: 47. Which portion of the nail apparatus is responsible for generating the dorsal nail plate?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Proximal matrix.
The proximal matrix is responsible for generating the dorsal nail plate.
Incorrect Answers:
A. The hyponychium is located close to the free edge of the nail.
C. The distal matrix is synonymous to the nail bed.
D. The nail bed is on the opposite side of the ventral nail plate.
E. The onychodermal band is also located close to the free edge of the nail.
Reference:
Histology, Nail.

question: 48. All of the following choices are factors determining the rate of growth for the nails. Which one represents a factor of slower growth rate?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
C. Onychomycosis.
The only choice that represents a factor that determines a slower growth rate is
onychomycosis. All other choices are associated with a faster growth rate.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 49. Select the term in the following choices that is the accurate terminology for rough nails:
explanation:
Correct Answer:
C. Trachyonychia.
Trachyonychia is the accurante terminology for rough nails.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Beau’s lines are transverse lines in the form of sulci (depressions).
B. Koilonychia represents spoon-shaped nails.
D. Onychoschizia refers to lamellar nail splitting (when the distal portion of the nail splits
horizontally).
E. Onychomadesis is the spontaneous separation of the nail from the matrix area.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 50. A patient describes « electric pain » caused by a lesion after a minor trauma to the involved nail. Which of the following nail tumors is the most probable diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
A. Glomus cell tumor.
The glomus cell tumor of the nail unit are very common and diagnosis is greatly helped by
the typical « electric » pain caused by the lesion after minor trauma of the involved nail. All
other tumors are not caracteristically painful.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 51. When considering the mechanism of action, which antifungal treatment used for onychomycosis inhibits the epoxidation of squalene, an early step in the formation of ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
E. Terbinafine.
Terbinafine’s mechanism of action is based on the inhibition of the epoxidation of squalene,
an early step in the formation of ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane.
Incorrect Answer:
A. and C. Itraconazole and Ketoconazole block the cytochrome P-450 dependant
demethylation stage in the converstion of lanoesterol into ergosterol in the formation of the
fungal cell membrane.
B. Ciclopirox olamine does not interfere with sterol synthesis. It acts as a chelating agent
and primarily affects iron-dependant mitochondrial enzymes.
D. Griseofulvin is fungistatic, however the exact mechanism by which it inhibits the growth
of dermatophytes is not clear. It is thought to inhibit fungal cell mitosis and nuclear acid
synthesis. It also binds to and interferes with the function of spindle and cytoplasmic
microtubules by binding to alpha and beta tubulin. It binds to keratin in human cells, then
once it reaches the fungal site of action, it binds to fungal microtubes thus altering the
fungal process of mitosis.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.http://www.drugbank.ca/drugs/db00400

question: 52. Hand foot and mouth disease can result in a specific nail complication. Identify this complication in the following choices:
explanation:
Correct Answer:
A. Onychomadesis.
Onychomadesis is a known complication of hand foot and mouth disease (HFMD).
Incorrect Answers:
B. and C. Subungual exostoses, transitory nail plate hyperpigmentation are not features of
HFMD.
D. Hutchison’s sign is related to melanoma.
E. Splinter hemorrhages have many causes but not HFMD.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 53. Psoriasis has many features involving the nails. Out ot the following choices, which one is most frequently seen?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
D. Pitting.
In order of frequency in all psoriasitic patients, the main nail features are: - Pitting (correct
answer) - Discoloration - Onycholysis - Subungual hyperkeratosis - Nail plate dystrophy Splinter hemorrhages
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 55. Nephrotic syndrome can result in a specific nail change, select the choice reflecting that change:
explanation:
Correct Answer:
B. Muehrcke’s lines.
Muehrckes’ lines are described as two transverse bands of pallor in the nail bed in patients
with chronic severely low serum albumin of <2,2g/100 mL. They are also seen in nephrotic
syndrome, glomerulonephritis, liver disease and malnutrition. (A) Terry’s nails are classically
found in cirrhotic patients.
Incorrect Answers:
C. Absence of the lunula is seen in patient undergoing dialysis. It is the most common nail
finding in these patients and can manifest at any time during the patient’s hemodialysis
program.
D. Blue lunula is reported in approximately 10% of patients with Wilson’s disease.
E. Koilonychia is the most common nail finding in patients with hemochromatosis, but is also
seen in iron-deficiency anemia and other disorders.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 56. A triangular lunula is almost pathognomonic for which condition?
explanation:
Correct Answer:
D. Nail patella syndrome.
A triangular lunula is almost pathognomonic for nail patella syndrome.
Incorrect Answers:
A. B. C. and E. Other choices are not characterized by a triangular lunula.
Reference:
Baran R, de Berker DAR, Holzberg M, Thomas L. Baran and Dawber's Diseases of the Nails and their
Management 4th Edition. July 2012, Wiley-Blackwell.

question: 57. A 40-year-old HIV positive patient presents with transverse white patches subungually near the proximal nail folds. What is the most likely causative organism of this condition?
explanation:
Correct Answer: D. Trichophyton rubrum.
The vignette describes a patient with proximal subungual onychomycosis, caused by
Trichophyton rubrum. Proximal subungual onychomycosis is the rarest form of
onychomycosis. It presents as a white to beige opacity on the proximal nail plate. The
incidence of proximal subungual onychomycosis is more than 5 times more common in HIV
positive individuals than in the immunocompetent population. Proximal subungual
onychomycosis has been considered a marker for HIV, and patients who present with this
disease should be screened for HIV.
Incorrect Answers:
A/B/C. These may be causes of distolateral subungal onychomycosis, the most common
form of onychomycosis.
E. Trichophyton interdigitale is the most common cause of the white superficial type of
onychomycosis, which results from direct invasion of the dorsal nail plate.
Reference:
Epidemiology and prevalence of onychomycosis in HIV-positive individuals. Gupta AK, Taborda P, Taborda
V, Gilmour J, Rachlis A, Salit I, Gupta MA, MacDonald P, Cooper EA, Summerbell RC. Int J Dermatol. 2000
Oct; 39(10):746-53.Superficial Fungal Infection, Amit Garg and Stefan Schieke. In: Goldsmith LA, Katz SI,
Gilchrest BA, Paller AS, Leffell DJ, Wolff K. eds. Fitzpatrick's Dermatology in General Medicine, 8th
edition. New York, NY: McGraw-Hill; 2012.

question: 58. A 7-year-old child presents to clinic with the following nail findings, which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: C. Repetitive nail trauma.
The patient presents with a medial depression in the bilateral thumbnails, with overlying
transverse ridging and an enlarged lunula. The most likely diagnosis is habit-tic deformity,
which results from the repeated rubbing of the proximal nail fold with the adjacent finger.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Nail–patella syndrome is caused by a mutation in LMX1B. Nails are absent or hypoplastic
and there is a characteristic triangularly shaped lunula.
B. Darier’s disease is caused by a mutation in ATP2A2, nail findings include red and white
longitudinal streaks with V shaped notching at the distal margin. Acrokeratosis verruciformis
of Hopf is a localized disorder of keratinization also related to ATP2A2 mutation that affects
the distal extremities but spares the nails.
D. Mutation in keratin 6a/16 results in pachyonychia congenital type I. Nails are thickened
(pachyonychia) with an increased curvature.
E. A digital mucous cyst can result in a depression in the nail plate distal to the cyst.
Typically a digital mucous cyst affects only one finger, and usually a cyst is visible on
examination.
Reference:
Habit-tic deformity.
Acrokeratosis verruciformis of Hopf is caused by mutation in ATP2A2: evidence that it is allelic to Darier's
disease

question: 59. A patient with Fitzpatrick skin type V presents to clinic for removal of unwanted hair. A laser using which of the following wavelengths would be best in this patient?
explanation:
Correct Answer: E. 1064 nm.
Laser hair removal employs lasers that target melanin within the hair follicle. In patients with
dark skin, epidermal melanin may also absorb laser energy leading to damage and
potentially dyspigmentation or scarring. For this reason, early-generation lasers were
contraindicated in patients with skin type IV and above. Lasers with longer wavelengths
penetrate deeper into the dermis and have less epidermal absorption, making them safer
for use in patients with dark skin. The Nd:YAG 1064 nm laser has been used safely for hair
removal in patients with darkly pigmented skin.
Incorrect Answers:
A/B/C.The pulse dye laser 585 nm, the ruby laser 694 nm, and the alexandrite 755 nm
would not be the best choice for a patient with dark skin due to the risk of damaging
epidermal melanin.
D. The diode laser 810 nm has been used for hair removal in dark skin, however
complications such as hypopigmentation have been reported. The Nd:YAG has a lower
incidence of adverse events, making it the gold standard for hair removal in dark skin.
Reference:
Alexis AF. Lasers and light-based therapies in ethnic skin: treatment options and recommendations for
Fitzpatrick skin types V and VI. Br J Dermatol. 2013 Oct;169 Suppl 3:91-7. Breadon JY, Barnes CA.
Comparison of adverse events of laser and light-assisted hair removal systems in skin types IV–VI. J
Drugs Dermatol 2007; 6:40–6.

question: 60. A 56-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of a darkened right great toenail. The nail has become misshapen, discolored, and thickened, with splitting of the nail plate. While there is no pain, she is upset about the appearance and would like treatment. What is the mechanism of action of the first line oral treatment for the condition noted above?

choices.C: Inhibition of aminoacyl-transfer ribonucleic acid synthetase
choices.E: Disruption of synthesis of DNA, RNA and protein

explanation:
Onychomycosis refers to fungal infection of the nails and can be caused by a variety of
organisms, including dermatophytes, yeast and mold. Most of the infections of the nail are
caused by the dermatophyte Trichophyton rubrum, which causes the condition known as
tinea unguium in 60-70% of cases. Candida species is the most common yeast associated
with onychomycosis. Fungi produce proteases that degrade keratin, which is thought to
facilitate invasion. Nail injury or nail disease reduces barrier protection and may increase the
risk of fungal invasion. Diagnostic tests include KOH preparations, histopathologic
examination of nail clippings with a PAS stain, fungal culture, and polymerase chain
reaction.
Treatment includes topical and systemic antifungal medications, laser, surgery, and
photodynamic therapy. For mild to moderate dermatophyte related onychomycosis,
terbinafine is the first line oral agent. Terbinafine inhibits squalene epoxidase, which is an
enzyme required for sterol biosynthesis. The inhibition of the epoxidase causes a
deficiency in ergosterol in the cell membrane of the fungus, resulting in cell death.
Incorrect Answers:
A. Lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase is involved in the ergosterol biosynthesis as well, and
disruption also results in cell membrane instability and death. This is the mechanism of
action of efinaconazole, which is a topical medication that can be used in the treatment of
onychomycosis and is not an oral therapy.
B. Itraconazole works by interfering with the cytochrome P450, which reduces the synthesis
of ergosterol (which is a key component of the fungal cell membrane). However, this is a
second line medication in individuals who cannot tolerate terbinafine orally.
C. Fungal protein synthesis inhibition through blocking of aminoacyl-transfer ribonucleic
acid synthetase is the mechanism of action for tavaborole, which can be used for the
treatment of onychomycosis, but it is also a topical agent.
E. This is the mechanism of action of ciclopirox, which can also be used to treat
onychomycosis but is not the first line drug.
Vital Concept:
Oral terbinafine is the first line treatment for onychomycosis. It inhibits squalene epoxidase.
Ergosterol is the main sterol in the cell membrane of fungi, and deficiency of squalene
epoxidase results in cell membrane dysfunction and cell death. A number of medications
work through the sterol synthesis disruption.
Reference:
Onychomycosis: Epidemiology, clinical features, and diagnosis.
Onychomycosis: Management.
Terbinafine (systemic): Drug information.
Tavaborole: Drug information.
Efinaconazole: Drug information.
Itraconazole: Drug information.
